content.problemStatement:
Rossiana Gones is on another adventure where he (yes, he) has stumbled upon an ancient cave. There are some ancient gold coins scattered across the cave in some of the cells of the cave. Given the layout of the cave, and Rossiana’s initial cell position, you will have to compute the maximum number of coins he can collect, and minimize the number of moves to do so. That is, the highest priority is to collect the maximum number of coins, and given that, you want to minimize the number of moves.

Description of the cave:Size and dimensions:The cave is cubical in shape of dimension H×L×WH \times L \times WH×L×W, where HHH is the height, LLL is the length and WWW is the width of the cave.

Each cell position is denoted by the coordinates (x,y,z)(x, y, z)(x,y,z).

Here,


x=1x = 1x=1 and x=Hx = Hx=H represent the lowest and highest level respectively.
y=1y = 1y=1 and y=Ly = Ly=L represent the west-most and east-most cells respectively.
z=1z = 1z=1 and z=Wz = Wz=W represent the south-most and north-most cells respectively.
So, the coordinates of the cell with the lowest level in the west-most and south-most position is (1,1,1)(1, 1, 1)(1,1,1) and the coordinates of the cell with the highest level in the east-most and north-most position is (H,L,W)(H, L, W)(H,L,W).

Floors and walls:Each cell might or might not have a floor, and the cells might or might not have walls surrounding it in any of the four directions i.e., north, east, south, and west.

Ladders:Some of the walls have extended brick ladders which can be used for climbing.

Adjacent cells:Two cells are adjacent if they share a side.

Gold coins:There are MMM ancient gold coins scattered across the cave in some of the cells of the cave. Each cell will contain at most one gold coin.

Description of the moves:Each possible move is defined as follows:


**Walk between adjacent cells:** You can move from one cell to an adjacent cell on the same level via walking, iff both cells have floors and there is no direct wall between them.
**Climb on a laddered wall in the same cell:** While standing on the floor of a cell, you can climb any existing laddered wall facing any direction in the same cell.
**Climbing down to the ladder on the wall below:** While standing on the floor of a cell, you can climb down to a laddered wall on a cell on the lower level which is one unit in front of the current cell in any direction if:

There is no floor on the cell one unit in front in that direction on the same level as the current one.
There is no wall between the current cell and the cell one unit in front on the same level.
The laddered wall on the lower-leveled cell is facing the opposite direction.


**Move to a laddered wall in an adjacent direction within the same cell:** While climbing on a laddered wall, you can climb to a laddered wall facing an adjacent direction in the same cell, if it exists. Note that you can’t climb from one laddered wall to another one in the same cell facing the opposite direction in the same move.
**Climb to a laddered wall in an adjacent cell:** While climbing on a laddered wall, you can climb to an adjacent laddered wall facing the same direction in an adjacent cell (not necessarily on the same level), iff there is no direct wall or floors between the cells which prevent you from moving. Note that, you are allowed to make sideway movements while on a ladder.
**Landing on a floor:** While climbing on a laddered wall, you can land on the floor of the same cell, if it exists.
**From a ladder, climb to the floor above:** While climbing on a laddered wall, you can climb up to the cell on the upper level which is one unit in front, facing the same direction if:

A floor exists on that cell.
There is no floor on the cell directly above the current one, i.e., the current cell doesn’t have a roof.
There is no direct wall between the two upper-level cells.


A gold coin may only be collected by standing on the floor of that gold coin’s cell. These cells are guaranteed to have floors. Also, the initial cell position will never have a gold coin.


Input Format
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT (1≤T≤10)(1 \leq T \leq 10)(1≤T≤10), which denotes the number of test cases. This is followed by the test cases.


The first line of each test case has three space-separated integers HHH, LLL, and WWW (2≤H,L,W≤25)(2 \leq H, L, W \leq 25)(2≤H,L,W≤25).


The second line contains the coordinates of the initial cell position aaa, bbb, and ccc (1≤a≤H,1≤b≤L,1≤c≤W)(1 \leq a \leq H, 1 \leq b \leq L, 1 \leq c \leq W)(1≤a≤H,1≤b≤L,1≤c≤W).


The next H×L×WH \times L \times WH×L×W lines contain the layout of each cell in increasing order of height, length, and then width. Each line contains five characters in the format: `F N E S W`, which represent the floor, the northern wall, the eastern wall, the southern wall, and the western wall respectively.



`F` can be either `#`, `C`, or `.`, which means a floor exists, a floor where a coin exists, and a floor doesn’t exist in that cell respectively.
The walls (`N`, `E`, `S`, `W`) can be either `#`, `L`, or `.`, which means a regular wall exists, a laddered wall exists, and a wall doesn’t exist in the respective direction for that cell respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output the case number, followed by two space-separated integers which represent the maximum number of coins Rossiana can collect and the minimum number of moves he can collect them in respectively.
constraints: The number of coins in the entire cave is at most 13 (1≤M≤13)
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T (1≤T≤10), which denotes the number of test cases. This is followed by the test cases. The first line of each test case has three space-separated integers H, L, and W (2≤H,L,W≤25). The second line contains the coordinates of the initial cell position a, b, and c (1≤a≤H,1≤b≤L,1≤c≤W). The next H×L×W lines contain the layout of each cell in increasing order of height, length, and then width. Each line contains five characters in the format: `F N E S W`, which represent the floor, the northern wall, the eastern wall, the southern wall, and the western wall respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the case number, followed by two space-separated integers which represent the maximum number of coins Rossiana can collect and the minimum number of moves he can collect them in respectively.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: The sample input consists of two test cases. In the first test case, Rossiana can collect 1 coin in 3 moves. In the second test case, Rossiana can collect 3 coins in 17 moves.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a 3D BFS algorithm to explore all possible paths from the initial cell position. We need to keep track of the maximum number of coins collected and the minimum number of moves required to collect them. We can use a queue to store the cells to be visited, and a 3D array to store the visited cells and the number of coins collected.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*H*L*W*26) — where 26 is the maximum number of possible moves from a cell (6 directions + 20 possible ladder moves)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(H*L*W) — for the 3D array to store the visited cells and the number of coins collected
